title: 1 1 Partial Differentiation
body:
TOPIC 1 : FUNCTIONS OF SEVERAL VARIABLES 1.1 PARTIAL DIFFERENTIATION

The definition of partial differentiation: The partial derivative of z(x, y) with respect to x and y is defined as

‚ąāz z(x + ‚ąÜx, y) ‚ąí z(x, y) ‚ąāz z(x, y + ‚ąÜy) ‚ąí z(x, y) = zx = lim , = zy = lim ‚ąāx ‚ąÜx‚ąí‚Üí0 ‚ąÜx ‚ąāy ‚ąÜy‚ąí‚Üí0 ‚ąÜy

In your previous studies, you have studied functions of a single variable y(x) and their rate of change dy/dx. In Engineering and the Sciences, we often need to deal with complicated systems that depend on many variables. In these lectures, we will introduce partial differentiation, which extends the familiar rules of calculus for functions of a single variable to functions of several variables.

For example, the function z(x, y) depends on two variables, x and y. We denote the rate of change of z in the x and y directions as ‚ąāz ‚ąāz = zx and = zy . ‚ąāx ‚ąāy The formal definition of these derivatives is given above but in practice all we need to remember is that the partial derivative treats all other variables exactly as if they were constant. ‚ąāz ‚ąāz Example 1 Find and if z = x2 + y 5 + 7. ‚ąāx ‚ąāy

‚ąāz ‚ąāz F = 2x, = 5y 4 F ‚ąāx ‚ąāy

Rules for differentiation

Most of the rules for ordinary derivatives carry straight over to partial derivatives.

y y0 xn nxn‚ąí1 eax aeax sin(ax) a cos(ax) cos(ax) ‚ąía sin(ax) ln(ax) x sinh(ax) a cosh(ax) cosh(ax) a sinh(ax)

Plotting functions of several variables Recall that the graph of a function of one variable y = f (x) is a curve in two-dimensional space R2 . The graph of a function of two variables z = f (x, y) is a surface in threedimensional space R3 .

Example 5 Sketch each of the following surfaces in space:

a) 3x + 4y + 6z = 12

b) x2 + y 2 + z 2 = 25

c) x2 + y 2 = 9

p d) z = 3 x2 + y 2

e) z = x2 + y 2

f ) x = y2 + z2

SUMMARY a) The co-ordinate axes x,y,z form a right-handed system.

b) ax + by + cz = d is a plane.

c) x2 + y 2 + z 2 = r2 is a sphere centred on the origin with radius r.

d) If a variable is missing then the function is independent of this variable. Extend the two dimensional curve to infinity in this direction. p 1 e) z = őĪ x2 + y 2 is a cone with semi-vertical angle tan‚ąí1 ( ). őĪ f ) z = őĪ(x2 + y 2 ) is a paraboloid of revolution.

Geometrical interpretation of partial derivatives

We do not need to restrict ourselves to functions of just two variables!

Higher order derivatives

As with ordinary derivatives, we can define second and higher order partial derivatives.

Example 7 If z = f (x, y) = x2 sin(y) + x3 y + y 5 find ‚ąāz = zx = ‚ąāx

‚ąāz = zy = ‚ąāy

  ‚ąā ‚ąāz = = zxx = ‚ąāx ‚ąāx ‚ąāx

  ‚ąā ‚ąāz = = zyy = ‚ąāy 2 ‚ąāy ‚ąāy

  ‚ąā ‚ąāz = = zyx = ‚ąāx‚ąāy ‚ąāx ‚ąāy

  ‚ąā ‚ąāz = = zxy = ‚ąāy‚ąāx ‚ąāy ‚ąāx

Notice that in the shorthand notation zxy , we first calculate the x derivative and then calculate the y derivative. In this example, you will observe that the mixed derivatives zxy and zyx give the same answer. This will generally be true for most well-behaved functions, in which case it does not matter what order you take the derivatives. It is not always true however! You can now do Q‚Äôs 1 to 4
